### Page layouts / front components owned by the POP Creations CRM app (audited 2026-06-02)
The app owns 4 `frontComponent` rows, 2 `pageLayout` rows + 4 tabs + 4 widgets, 1 command-menu
item, 1 role, 24 files. Findings:

- **Component code is all in the repo, consolidated** in `twenty-front/src/{pages,modules}/pop-creations`
  (`MondayMorningDashboardPage`, `DepartmentDashboardWidget`, `ProgramFolioWidget`, `widgetRegistry`).
  There is **no duplicate/SDK copy** in `packages/twenty-apps` to merge — nothing to consolidate.
- **`frontComponent` row usage:**
  - `DepartmentDashboard` (dfe52185) → referenced by the "Department Dashboard" widget — **used**
  - `ProgramFolio` (471f26c4) → referenced by the "Folio" widget — **used**
  - `MondayMorningDashboard` (982c22cd) → referenced by a **command-menu item** — **used, keep**
  - `PersonDepartmentPicker` (6c302daf) → **0 references, no code** → **vestigial, safe to drop**
    (its function was absorbed into the native relation/department picker work).
- ❌ **The 2 page layouts are DEAD — confirmed not rendering on any record.** Proof:
  `getRecordPageLayoutId.ts` resolves a record's layout from `record.pageLayoutId` (a per-record
  override) or a hardcoded object→default-layout map. **`department`/`factory`/`opportunity` are not
  in that map** (only company/person/opportunity/note/task/workflow…), and their record tables
  **have no `pageLayoutId` column** (only `dashboard` does). Combined with `objectMetadataId = NULL`
  on both layouts, nothing ever selects them. So the "Department Record Page" / "Program Record Page"
  layouts + their `DepartmentDashboard`/`ProgramFolio` widgets are **orphaned drift** — built (likely
  in the layout editor) but never wired to display.
  > **Heads-up:** the `DepartmentDashboardWidget` / `ProgramFolioWidget` you built are **not
  > currently shown** on department/program records. If you want them visible, that's a small
  > *new* wiring task in v2.8 (a `DefaultDepartmentRecordPageLayout.ts` in the existing pattern),
  > not drift to preserve. The only live custom front-component is **MondayMorningDashboard**
  > (command-menu item + native `/pop-creations` route — fully code-backed).

**Re-fork recommendation — do NOT capture page layouts in a SQL migration.** v2.8 reworked the
page-layout system (259 files); v1.20-shaped `pageLayout`/`pageLayoutTab`/`pageLayoutWidget` rows
(especially with NULL object linkage) will not port cleanly. The durable path is to reproduce the
dashboard mounting **in code** during the re-fork — either as code-defined record layouts
(the `Default*RecordPageLayout.ts` pattern, which exists for company/opportunity/note/task but not
department/program) or via the v2.x SDK app manifest (`definePageLayoutTab`). The mounting spec to
recreate: **Department record → "Overview" tab → DepartmentDashboard widget + "Activity" tab →
Timeline; Program record → "Folio" tab → ProgramFolio widget + "Activity" tab → Timeline.** The
component code already exists; only the placement needs re-expressing.

**Drift remediation — final status:** data model ✅ code; saved views/nav ✅ migrations `010`/`011`;
`creditStatus` ⚪ intentionally not captured (unused); `PersonDepartmentPicker` frontComponent
✅ dropped (migration `012`); the 2 dead page layouts + their widgets ⚪ harmless orphans (not
rendered) — re-mount in code at re-fork only if the dashboards are wanted; `MondayMorningDashboard`
✅ code-backed + reachable. **No remaining drift affects a re-fork's correctness.**

The email router + outlook ingestion are the IP and stay.

## 14. Deep-dive: contact-creation verification + Workflow mapping

### 14a. ⚠️ CORRECTION: outlook-ingest / email-contact-sync / clickup-sync are LIVE, not stubs
**An earlier draft of §13–§15 wrongly called these three "dead stubs." They are live.** The
mistake: each name has TWO files — an unused placeholder `logic-functions/<name>/services/<name>.service.ts`
(which *was* a "In a real implementation…" stub) **and** the real implementation in
`crons/jobs/<name>.cron.job.ts` (registered in `pop-creations.module.ts`, actively running). The
placeholder dirs were unreferenced dead code and have been **deleted**; the cron jobs are untouched.

| Cron job | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `outlook-ingest.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE & CRITICAL** | polls Microsoft Graph every 15 min; **100% of the 10,507 `_emailMessage` rows have `outlookMessageId`** (all sourced here), latest `receivedAt` = today. **This is the email-ingestion pipeline** — it also calls the router + SO/PO extractor + AI summary on each email. |
| `email-contact-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE** | daily 2am; groups external email domains → finds/creates Company + Person (subdomain matching aligned with the router, noise-domain filtering). |
| `clickup-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE (idle)** | daily 7am; real ClickUp API sync — but **0 opportunities exist**, so nothing to act on. |

**Implication:** these are NOT delete candidates. `outlook-ingest` is tightly coupled to the router
(it writes the custom `emailMessage` object that the router classifies), so swapping to the native
Microsoft driver is a re-architecture, not a drop-in — keep it. Only the unused placeholder service
files were removed.

### 14b. Contact-creation: native is a *partial* overlap, not a drop-in
The **`email-contact-sync.cron.job`** is real (creates companies/people by email domain). v2.8's
`CreateCompanyAndPersonService` does similar from native messaging, but our version operates on the
custom `emailMessage` object and shares domain/subdomain logic with the router. So it is coupled to
our pipeline — treat native contact-creation as a *future* consolidation to evaluate at re-fork, not
a zero-risk drop. (The earlier "custom was a stub / drop with zero risk" claim was wrong.)

### 14c. Stage/automation listeners → native Workflows (DATABASE_EVENT trigger + actions)
v2.8 Workflows support `DATABASE_EVENT` (record created/updated/deleted), `CRON`, `WEBHOOK`,
`MANUAL` triggers and `CREATE_RECORD` / `UPDATE_RECORD` / `CODE` / `AI_AGENT` / `SEND_EMAIL` /
`HTTP_REQUEST` actions. All four custom `@OnEvent` listeners map cleanly:

| Custom listener | Native Workflow trigger | Filter | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `program-stage-change` | Record Updated · `opportunity` | `stage` ∈ {PROPOSAL, NEGOTIATION} | Create Record · `licensorApprovalThread` (linked to the opportunity) |
| `lat-stage-follow-up` | Record Updated · `licensorApprovalThread` | `stage` ∈ follow-up set | Create Record · `task` (linked to the LAT) |
| `new-program-tasks` | Record Created · `opportunity` | — | Create Record · `task` × 6 (the checklist templates), each linked |
| `contact-auto-scope` | Record Created · `person` | — | Update Record · set `scope` (DEPARTMENT / COMPANY_WIDE / IGNORED) — use a Code action for the branch, or 2 filtered workflows |

**Bonus — migrating fixes a latent bug.** `program-stage-change` and `lat-stage-follow-up` both do
`const { stage: newStage, stage: previousStage } = properties;` — destructuring the **same** field
twice, so their `if (newStage === previousStage) return;` guard can never be false and the action
**likely never fires today**. A native Workflow "updated → field changed to X" trigger removes the
bug entirely.

**Caveat — workflows are workspace data.** A no-code Workflow lives in the DB (like views), so to
keep it reproducible on re-fork it should be **defined in an app manifest** (the `twenty-fireflies`
app ships workflow-builder actions this way) rather than clicked together in the UI. Net trade:
~4 custom backend listeners (incl. a bug) → declarative workflow definitions in the POP Creations
app manifest.

### Summary of the fork-reduction opportunity (corrected)
- **Deleted (unreferenced placeholder dirs, zero loss):** the stub service files under
  `logic-functions/{outlook-ingest,email-contact-sync,clickup-sync}/`. The **cron jobs of the same
  names are LIVE and were kept.**
- **Genuinely dead — rebuild as native Workflows (post-v2.8, manifest-defined):**
  `program-stage-change`, `lat-stage-follow-up`, `new-program-tasks`, `contact-auto-scope` — 4
  unregistered listeners → declarative workflows (fixes the latent bug too).
- **Live — keep:** `outlook-ingest` (email pipeline), `email-contact-sync`, `clickup-sync` (idle),
  `email-router` / `email-rerouter` / `so-extractor`, `fireflies-ingest`, custom objects + fields.
- **Future consolidation to evaluate at re-fork (not drop-in):** Outlook ingest → native Microsoft
  driver; contact creation → `contact-creation-manager`; Fireflies → `twenty-fireflies`; AI summary
  → AI agent action — each is coupled to the custom email object/router, so validate before adopting.

---

## 15. VERIFIED: which custom features are silently dead in production (2026-06-02)

Verified by (1) code mechanism and (2) live production data in `workspace_93r34ew9zc9644a9y5f1yeylz`.

**Root cause for the automations:** the four standalone listeners subscribe with
`@OnEvent('opportunity.updated', …)` and read a **single-record** payload
(`payload.recordId`, `payload.properties.stage`). But the platform emits **batched** events
(`computeEventName` → `'opportunity.updated'`, payload = `WorkspaceEventBatch{ events: [...] }`,
each event having `.recordId` and `.properties.{before, after}`). The correct pattern —
`@OnDatabaseBatchEvent('opportunity', UPDATED)` with `payload.events[]` — is what the working main
listener and all native listeners use. So the four custom listeners fire but read `undefined`
fields off the batch (the stage ones then double-destructure `properties.stage`), crash into their
own try/catch, and **no-op**.

| Custom feature | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `outlook-ingest.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE & CRITICAL** | 10,507/10,507 emails have `outlookMessageId`; latest `receivedAt` = today — the whole email pipeline |
| `email-router` / `email-rerouter` | ✅ **WORKING** | 7,293 routed to a company (`COMPANY_ONLY`); 2,621 UNROUTED, 593 SKIPPED; `_emailMessage_reroute_backup` present |
| `email-contact-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE** | companies/people created from email domains (mostly since re-classified off `UNASSIGNED`) |
| `clickup-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE (idle)** | real ClickUp sync, but **0 opportunities** exist so nothing to do |
| `new-program-tasks` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | listener module not registered + 0 opportunities; 0 template tasks |
| `contact-auto-scope` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered; **all 8,602** people have `scope = NULL` |
| `program-stage-change` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered + wrong payload shape; 0 `_licensorApprovalThread` rows |
| `lat-stage-follow-up` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered + wrong payload shape; no LATs exist |

**Corrected count: the 4 `@OnEvent` automation *listeners* are dead** (their modules are never
imported, so they're never instantiated; the two stage ones also misread the batch payload). The
three `cron.job` features (`outlook-ingest`, `email-contact-sync`, `clickup-sync`) are **LIVE** —
the only "stubs" were unused placeholder service files (now deleted). `clickup-sync` and the
opportunity-triggered automations also have **0 opportunities** to act on.

**Implication for the re-fork:** keep the live cron jobs (especially `outlook-ingest`). Re-implement
the 4 dead automations as native Workflows (§14c) — they will work *for the first time*.

**⚠️ Context — the system is PRE-LAUNCH, not live.** The empty production data (0 opportunities,
0 LATs, all people unscoped) reflects that the CRM has **not gone live yet** and not everything
specced is exercised — it is **not** evidence of abandonment. The opportunity / program / LAT
features and their four automations are **intended functionality**. So the decision is settled:
**rebuild all four as native Workflows** (do not delete). They were specced, were never wired up
correctly, and have simply never had data flow through them yet.

**Decision (in the plan): rebuild all 4 automations as native, manifest-defined Workflows at the
re-fork** — `program-stage-change`, `lat-stage-follow-up`, `new-program-tasks`, `contact-auto-scope`.
The native Workflow engine already exists (even in v1.20) and is the correct mechanism; the custom
`@OnEvent` listeners were a reinvention that never ran. See Part 2 · Phase E-bis below.

      This command sets up your project if needed. The installation is split into four different steps that each have their own characteristics:

      - **Resolution:** First the package manager will resolve your dependencies. The exact way a dependency version is privileged over another isn't standardized outside of the regular semver guarantees. If a package doesn't resolve to what you would expect, check that all dependencies are correctly declared (also check our website for more information: ).

      - **Fetch:** Then we download all the dependencies if needed, and make sure that they're all stored within our cache (check the value of `cacheFolder` in `yarn config` to see where the cache files are stored).

      - **Link:** Then we send the dependency tree information to internal plugins tasked with writing them on the disk in some form (for example by generating the `.pnp.cjs` file you might know).

      - **Build:** Once the dependency tree has been written on the disk, the package manager will now be free to run the build scripts for all packages that might need it, in a topological order compatible with the way they depend on one another. See https://yarnpkg.com/advanced/lifecycle-scripts for detail.

      Note that running this command is not part of the recommended workflow. Yarn supports zero-installs, which means that as long as you store your cache and your `.pnp.cjs` file inside your repository, everything will work without requiring any install right after cloning your repository or switching branches.

      If the `--immutable` option is set (defaults to true on CI), Yarn will abort with an error exit code if the lockfile was to be modified (other paths can be added using the `immutablePatterns` configuration setting). For backward compatibility we offer an alias under the name of `--frozen-lockfile`, but it will be removed in a later release.

      If the `--immutable-cache` option is set, Yarn will abort with an error exit code if the cache folder was to be modified (either because files would be added, or because they'd be removed).

      If the `--refresh-lockfile` option is set, Yarn will keep the same resolution for the packages currently in the lockfile but will refresh their metadata. If used together with `--immutable`, it can validate that the lockfile information are consistent. This flag is enabled by default when Yarn detects it runs within a pull request context.

      If the `--check-cache` option is set, Yarn will always refetch the packages and will ensure that their checksum matches what's 1/ described in the lockfile 2/ inside the existing cache files (if present). This is recommended as part of your CI workflow if you're both following the Zero-Installs model and accepting PRs from third-parties, as they'd otherwise have the ability to alter the checked-in packages before submitting them.

      If the `--inline-builds` option is set, Yarn will verbosely print the output of the build steps of your dependencies (instead of writing them into individual files). This is likely useful mostly for debug purposes only when using Docker-like environments.

      If the `--mode=<mode>` option is set, Yarn will change which artifacts are generated. The modes currently supported are:

      - `skip-build` will not run the build scripts at all. Note that this is different from setting `enableScripts` to false because the latter will disable build scripts, and thus affect the content of the artifacts generated on disk, whereas the former will just disable the build step - but not the scripts themselves, which just won't run.

      - `update-lockfile` will skip the link step altogether, and only fetch packages that are missing from the lockfile (or that have no associated checksums). Th…12316 tokens truncated…mmand is `run` and the script being run does not exist the child workspace will be skipped without error.

    94	## 5. The `messageChannel` / `messageFolder` / `calendarChannel` change
    95	
    96	**What upstream did** (commit `76e144e85a`, PR #20836; migration command
    97	`upgrade:2-8:drop-channel-standard-objects`): these three were **standard per-workspace CRM
    98	objects** (visible in the data-model UI, queryable via GraphQL, with views & page-layouts).
    99	They are now **core metadata-module entities** (`engine/metadata-modules/message-channel/…`) —
   100	internal system tables. The upgrade command **drops the redundant workspace-schema copies**.
   101	Sync functionality is unchanged; emails/calendars still sync exactly as before.
   102	
   103	**Do we use them?** **No — not functionally.** Our routing operates entirely on
   104	`message` / `_emailMessage`, `messageParticipant`, `company`, and `person`. There are
   105	**zero functional references** to the three objects in our 230 commits (the only mentions are
   106	in a passive metadata-schema snapshot JSON). Our router routes by *sender domain → company*, so
   107	it never needed to know which inbox an email arrived through.
   108	
   109	**Better / worse?**
   110	- **Can we still do everything we do? Yes, fully.** We never built on the user-facing object.
   111	- **Better:** the messaging subsystem we extend got real upgrades (send-from-UI, forwarding
   112	  channel, internal-email sync toggle, reply-account-from-thread, credential-change sync reset,
   113	  channel indexing for sync crons, dedicated Email settings page).

   118	    (Our committed code does not — verified — but prod may hold ad-hoc objects the repo doesn't capture.)

   725	## 14. Deep-dive: contact-creation verification + Workflow mapping
   726	
   727	### 14a. ⚠️ CORRECTION: outlook-ingest / email-contact-sync / clickup-sync are LIVE, not stubs
   728	**An earlier draft of §13–§15 wrongly called these three "dead stubs." They are live.** The
   729	mistake: each name has TWO files — an unused placeholder `logic-functions/<name>/services/<name>.service.ts`
   730	(which *was* a "In a real implementation…" stub) **and** the real implementation in
   731	`crons/jobs/<name>.cron.job.ts` (registered in `pop-creations.module.ts`, actively running). The
   732	placeholder dirs were unreferenced dead code and have been **deleted**; the cron jobs are untouched.
   733	
   734	| Cron job | Status | Evidence |
   735	|---|---|---|
   736	| `outlook-ingest.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE & CRITICAL** | polls Microsoft Graph every 15 min; **100% of the 10,507 `_emailMessage` rows have `outlookMessageId`** (all sourced here), latest `receivedAt` = today. **This is the email-ingestion pipeline** — it also calls the router + SO/PO extractor + AI summary on each email. |
   737	| `email-contact-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE** | daily 2am; groups external email domains → finds/creates Company + Person (subdomain matching aligned with the router, noise-domain filtering). |
   738	| `clickup-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE (idle)** | daily 7am; real ClickUp API sync — but **0 opportunities exist**, so nothing to act on. |
   739	
   740	**Implication:** these are NOT delete candidates. `outlook-ingest` is tightly coupled to the router
   741	(it writes the custom `emailMessage` object that the router classifies), so swapping to the native
   742	Microsoft driver is a re-architecture, not a drop-in — keep it. Only the unused placeholder service
   743	files were removed.
   744	
   745	### 14b. Contact-creation: native is a *partial* overlap, not a drop-in
   746	The **`email-contact-sync.cron.job`** is real (creates companies/people by email domain). v2.8's
   747	`CreateCompanyAndPersonService` does similar from native messaging, but our version operates on the
   748	custom `emailMessage` object and shares domain/subdomain logic with the router. So it is coupled to
   749	our pipeline — treat native contact-creation as a *future* consolidation to evaluate at re-fork, not
   750	a zero-risk drop. (The earlier "custom was a stub / drop with zero risk" claim was wrong.)
   751	
   752	### 14c. Stage/automation listeners → native Workflows (DATABASE_EVENT trigger + actions)
   753	v2.8 Workflows support `DATABASE_EVENT` (record created/updated/deleted), `CRON`, `WEBHOOK`,
   754	`MANUAL` triggers and `CREATE_RECORD` / `UPDATE_RECORD` / `CODE` / `AI_AGENT` / `SEND_EMAIL` /
   755	`HTTP_REQUEST` actions. All four custom `@OnEvent` listeners map cleanly:
   756	
   757	| Custom listener | Native Workflow trigger | Filter | Action |
   758	|---|---|---|---|
   759	| `program-stage-change` | Record Updated · `opportunity` | `stage` ∈ {PROPOSAL, NEGOTIATION} | Create Record · `licensorApprovalThread` (linked to the opportunity) |
   760	| `lat-stage-follow-up` | Record Updated · `licensorApprovalThread` | `stage` ∈ follow-up set | Create Record · `task` (linked to the LAT) |

Chunk ID: 359d93
Wall time: 0.0000 seconds
Process exited with code 0
Original token count: 1640
Output:
   760	| `lat-stage-follow-up` | Record Updated · `licensorApprovalThread` | `stage` ∈ follow-up set | Create Record · `task` (linked to the LAT) |
   761	| `new-program-tasks` | Record Created · `opportunity` | — | Create Record · `task` × 6 (the checklist templates), each linked |
   762	| `contact-auto-scope` | Record Created · `person` | — | Update Record · set `scope` (DEPARTMENT / COMPANY_WIDE / IGNORED) — use a Code action for the branch, or 2 filtered workflows |
   763	
   764	**Bonus — migrating fixes a latent bug.** `program-stage-change` and `lat-stage-follow-up` both do
   765	`const { stage: newStage, stage: previousStage } = properties;` — destructuring the **same** field
   766	twice, so their `if (newStage === previousStage) return;` guard can never be false and the action
   767	**likely never fires today**. A native Workflow "updated → field changed to X" trigger removes the
   768	bug entirely.
   769	
   770	**Caveat — workflows are workspace data.** A no-code Workflow lives in the DB (like views), so to
   771	keep it reproducible on re-fork it should be **defined in an app manifest** (the `twenty-fireflies`
   772	app ships workflow-builder actions this way) rather than clicked together in the UI. Net trade:
   773	~4 custom backend listeners (incl. a bug) → declarative workflow definitions in the POP Creations
   774	app manifest.
   775	
   776	### Summary of the fork-reduction opportunity (corrected)
   777	- **Deleted (unreferenced placeholder dirs, zero loss):** the stub service files under
   778	  `logic-functions/{outlook-ingest,email-contact-sync,clickup-sync}/`. The **cron jobs of the same
   779	  names are LIVE and were kept.**
   780	- **Genuinely dead — rebuild as native Workflows (post-v2.8, manifest-defined):**
   781	  `program-stage-change`, `lat-stage-follow-up`, `new-program-tasks`, `contact-auto-scope` — 4
   782	  unregistered listeners → declarative workflows (fixes the latent bug too).
   783	- **Live — keep:** `outlook-ingest` (email pipeline), `email-contact-sync`, `clickup-sync` (idle),
   784	  `email-router` / `email-rerouter` / `so-extractor`, `fireflies-ingest`, custom objects + fields.
   785	- **Future consolidation to evaluate at re-fork (not drop-in):** Outlook ingest → native Microsoft
   786	  driver; contact creation → `contact-creation-manager`; Fireflies → `twenty-fireflies`; AI summary
   787	  → AI agent action — each is coupled to the custom email object/router, so validate before adopting.
   788	
   789	---
   790	
   791	## 15. VERIFIED: which custom features are silently dead in production (2026-06-02)
   792	
   793	Verified by (1) code mechanism and (2) live production data in `workspace_93r34ew9zc9644a9y5f1yeylz`.
   794	
   795	**Root cause for the automations:** the four standalone listeners subscribe with
   796	`@OnEvent('opportunity.updated', …)` and read a **single-record** payload
   797	(`payload.recordId`, `payload.properties.stage`). But the platform emits **batched** events
   798	(`computeEventName` → `'opportunity.updated'`, payload = `WorkspaceEventBatch{ events: [...] }`,
   799	each event having `.recordId` and `.properties.{before, after}`). The correct pattern —
   800	`@OnDatabaseBatchEvent('opportunity', UPDATED)` with `payload.events[]` — is what the working main
   801	listener and all native listeners use. So the four custom listeners fire but read `undefined`
   802	fields off the batch (the stage ones then double-destructure `properties.stage`), crash into their
   803	own try/catch, and **no-op**.
   804	
   805	| Custom feature | Status | Evidence |
   806	|---|---|---|
   807	| `outlook-ingest.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE & CRITICAL** | 10,507/10,507 emails have `outlookMessageId`; latest `receivedAt` = today — the whole email pipeline |
   808	| `email-router` / `email-rerouter` | ✅ **WORKING** | 7,293 routed to a company (`COMPANY_ONLY`); 2,621 UNROUTED, 593 SKIPPED; `_emailMessage_reroute_backup` present |
   809	| `email-contact-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE** | companies/people created from email domains (mostly since re-classified off `UNASSIGNED`) |
   810	| `clickup-sync.cron.job` | ✅ **LIVE (idle)** | real ClickUp sync, but **0 opportunities** exist so nothing to do |
   811	| `new-program-tasks` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | listener module not registered + 0 opportunities; 0 template tasks |
   812	| `contact-auto-scope` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered; **all 8,602** people have `scope = NULL` |
   813	| `program-stage-change` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered + wrong payload shape; 0 `_licensorApprovalThread` rows |
   814	| `lat-stage-follow-up` (listener) | ❌ **DEAD** | module not registered + wrong payload shape; no LATs exist |
   815	
   816	**Corrected count: the 4 `@OnEvent` automation *listeners* are dead** (their modules are never
   817	imported, so they're never instantiated; the two stage ones also misread the batch payload). The
   818	three `cron.job` features (`outlook-ingest`, `email-contact-sync`, `clickup-sync`) are **LIVE** —
   819	the only "stubs" were unused placeholder service files (now deleted). `clickup-sync` and the
   820	opportunity-triggered automations also have **0 opportunities** to act on.
   821	
   822	**Implication for the re-fork:** keep the live cron jobs (especially `outlook-ingest`). Re-implement
   823	the 4 dead automations as native Workflows (§14c) — they will work *for the first time*.
   824	
   825	**⚠️ Context — the system is PRE-LAUNCH, not live.** The empty production data (0 opportunities,
   826	0 LATs, all people unscoped) reflects that the CRM has **not gone live yet** and not everything
   827	specced is exercised — it is **not** evidence of abandonment. The opportunity / program / LAT
   828	features and their four automations are **intended functionality**. So the decision is settled:
   829	**rebuild all four as native Workflows** (do not delete). They were specced, were never wired up
   830	correctly, and have simply never had data flow through them yet.
   831	
   832	**Decision (in the plan): rebuild all 4 automations as native, manifest-defined Workflows at the
   833	re-fork** — `program-stage-change`, `lat-stage-follow-up`, `new-program-tasks`, `contact-auto-scope`.
   834	The native Workflow engine already exists (even in v1.20) and is the correct mechanism; the custom
   835	`@OnEvent` listeners were a reinvention that never ran. See Part 2 · Phase E-bis below.

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Thread Coordination
- When the user asks to create, inspect, continue, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads in the background, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

payload.message:
I read [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:1). My take: the broad direction is right, but the execution plan is still too optimistic in the exact places that matter for your business-specific behavior.

**Findings**

1. **The plan under-scopes the custom data model reapply.**  
   Early sections say the `pop-creations` module is mostly net-new and low-conflict, but later the doc correctly admits that POP’s custom fields/objects are embedded in modified upstream standard-entity files and metadata utilities: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:569). That later correction needs to be promoted into the main execution plan. The custom schema is not just “copy the module wholesale.”

2. **“Manifest-defined Workflows” may not actually exist as assumed.**  
   The plan says to rebuild four dead listeners as native manifest-defined Workflows: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:425). I checked the local SDK/app definition surface and did not find a `defineWorkflow`-style API. Twenty apps appear to define logic functions/tools, not necessarily full declarative workflow graphs. Before depending on this, the plan needs a confirmed implementation path: app logic functions, UI-created workflows captured by migration, or a custom seed/upgrade command.

3. **Copying `pop-creations` wholesale is directionally fine, but “zero conflicts” is misleading.**  
   The module may not text-conflict with upstream, but it uses deep internal APIs: workspace ORM managers, system auth context, cron jobs, database event listeners, raw repositories, Microsoft Graph ingest, and custom object metadata. Those can break at compile/runtime even with no Git conflicts. Phase C/D should explicitly include import/API adaptation and end-to-end tests for Outlook ingest, routing, rerouting, contact sync, Fireflies, ClickUp, and opportunity summary behavior.

4. **The migration plan is too manual for production.**  
   Applying `001`-`012` directly with `psql` after Twenty’s own upgrade commands: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:494) is risky. Those migrations were written against the fork’s current schema, not necessarily a v2.8-upgraded schema. I’d convert them into idempotent registered upgrade commands or at least test every migration against a prod restore after the native Twenty upgrade. The doc calls out `008` and `010`, but all twelve need validation.

5. **Encryption/key handling is too simple.**  
   The plan says provision `ENCRYPTION_KEY`: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:453). For an existing deployment, this needs a concrete old-secret/new-secret migration path. Otherwise connected accounts, tokens, or secrets may become unreadable. Add a staging check that Google/Microsoft connected accounts still decrypt, sync, and send/receive after upgrade.

6. **The target version needs to be pinned and re-verified.**  
   The doc says start from `v2.8.0`: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:356). In the current local refs I could not confirm that tag cleanly; upstream refs/tags look stale or inconsistent. Before execution, fetch tags, verify the actual latest public Twenty release/head, and pin an exact commit SHA. Do not rely on a floating assumption like “latest v2.8”.

7. **The plan mixes two strategies: fork reapply vs app migration.**  
   It says keep/copy native module code, but also talks about SDK apps, manifest front components, and workflows. That’s not necessarily wrong, but the plan should name the chosen architecture. For your deep email/contact behavior, I think the realistic near-term path is: re-fork to v2.8, preserve POP internals, and only migrate low-risk UI/page/widget pieces toward apps later.

8. **The backup section is good, but needs a restore drill.**  
   The backup commands around [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:335) are useful, but add: restore the dump into staging and verify it boots before touching production. Also use one timestamp variable instead of calling `date` multiple times.

**What I Like**

The biggest good call is rejecting a normal merge: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:122). With this much divergence, re-forking and reapplying is the sane path.

I also like that the plan now treats Outlook ingest, email routing, email/contact sync, ClickUp sync, and Fireflies as live business behavior, not cleanup candidates: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:727). That matches what you clarified: these are the parts that embody how your business works.

**What I’d Change Before Executing**

Make the plan’s main checklist explicitly about preserving business behavior:

- Pin exact upstream commit/tag.
- Recreate POP data model first.
- Port `pop-creations` with compile/API adaptation.
- Keep live cron jobs/listeners unless a verified v2.8-native replacement exists.
- Do not assume workflows can be declared by SDK manifest until proven.
- Convert POP SQL migrations into idempotent upgrade steps.
- Add staging tests for deep email/contact flows, not just smoke tests.
- Treat frontend polish separately from core business behavior.

So: good strategic plan, but it needs to be tightened around the custom schema, workflow assumption, encryption migration, and email/contact end-to-end verification before I’d trust it for production.

7. **The plan mixes two strategies: fork reapply vs app migration.**  
   It says keep/copy native module code, but also talks about SDK apps, manifest front components, and workflows. That’s not necessarily wrong, but the plan should name the chosen architecture. For your deep email/contact behavior, I think the realistic near-term path is: re-fork to v2.8, preserve POP internals, and only migrate low-risk UI/page/widget pieces toward apps later.

8. **The backup section is good, but needs a restore drill.**  
   The backup commands around [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:335) are useful, but add: restore the dump into staging and verify it boots before touching production. Also use one timestamp variable instead of calling `date` multiple times.

**What I Like**

The biggest good call is rejecting a normal merge: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:122). With this much divergence, re-forking and reapplying is the sane path.

I also like that the plan now treats Outlook ingest, email routing, email/contact sync, ClickUp sync, and Fireflies as live business behavior, not cleanup candidates: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:727). That matches what you clarified: these are the parts that embody how your business works.

**What I’d Change Before Executing**

Make the plan’s main checklist explicitly about preserving business behavior:

- Pin exact upstream commit/tag.
- Recreate POP data model first.
- Port `pop-creations` with compile/API adaptation.
- Keep live cron jobs/listeners unless a verified v2.8-native replacement exists.
- Do not assume workflows can be declared by SDK manifest until proven.
- Convert POP SQL migrations into idempotent upgrade steps.
- Add staging tests for deep email/contact flows, not just smoke tests.
- Treat frontend polish separately from core business behavior.

So: good strategic plan, but it needs to be tightened around the custom schema, workflow assumption, encryption migration, and email/contact end-to-end verification before I’d trust it for production.

I read [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:1). My take: the broad direction is right, but the execution plan is still too optimistic in the exact places that matter for your business-specific behavior.

**Findings**

1. **The plan under-scopes the custom data model reapply.**  
   Early sections say the `pop-creations` module is mostly net-new and low-conflict, but later the doc correctly admits that POP’s custom fields/objects are embedded in modified upstream standard-entity files and metadata utilities: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:569). That later correction needs to be promoted into the main execution plan. The custom schema is not just “copy the module wholesale.”

2. **“Manifest-defined Workflows” may not actually exist as assumed.**  
   The plan says to rebuild four dead listeners as native manifest-defined Workflows: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:425). I checked the local SDK/app definition surface and did not find a `defineWorkflow`-style API. Twenty apps appear to define logic functions/tools, not necessarily full declarative workflow graphs. Before depending on this, the plan needs a confirmed implementation path: app logic functions, UI-created workflows captured by migration, or a custom seed/upgrade command.

3. **Copying `pop-creations` wholesale is directionally fine, but “zero conflicts” is misleading.**  
   The module may not text-conflict with upstream, but it uses deep internal APIs: workspace ORM managers, system auth context, cron jobs, database event listeners, raw repositories, Microsoft Graph ingest, and custom object metadata. Those can break at compile/runtime even with no Git conflicts. Phase C/D should explicitly include import/API adaptation and end-to-end tests for Outlook ingest, routing, rerouting, contact sync, Fireflies, ClickUp, and opportunity summary behavior.

4. **The migration plan is too manual for production.**  
   Applying `001`-`012` directly with `psql` after Twenty’s own upgrade commands: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:494) is risky. Those migrations were written against the fork’s current schema, not necessarily a v2.8-upgraded schema. I’d convert them into idempotent registered upgrade commands or at least test every migration against a prod restore after the native Twenty upgrade. The doc calls out `008` and `010`, but all twelve need validation.

5. **Encryption/key handling is too simple.**  
   The plan says provision `ENCRYPTION_KEY`: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:453). For an existing deployment, this needs a concrete old-secret/new-secret migration path. Otherwise connected accounts, tokens, or secrets may become unreadable. Add a staging check that Google/Microsoft connected accounts still decrypt, sync, and send/receive after upgrade.

6. **The target version needs to be pinned and re-verified.**  
   The doc says start from `v2.8.0`: [2.8_upgrade.md](/worksp/twenty/fork/2.8_upgrade.md:356). In the current local refs I could not confirm that tag cleanly; upstream refs/tags look stale or inconsistent. Before execution, fetch tags, verify the actual latest public Twenty release/head, and pin an exact commit SHA. Do not rely on a floating assumption like “latest v2.8”.

content.text:
<collaboration_mode># Plan Mode (Conversational)

You work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.

## Mode rules (strict)

You are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.

Plan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.

## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool

Plan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.

Separately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.

## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode

You may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.

### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)

Actions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs
* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration
* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files
* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files

### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)

Actions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Editing or writing files
* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files
* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files
* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it

When in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as "doing the work" rather than "planning the work," do not do it.

## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)

Begin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.

Before asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.

Exception: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.

Do not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, "where is this struct?" or "which UI component should we use?" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.

## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)

* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.
* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.

## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)

* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.

## Asking questions

Critical rules:

* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.
* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.
* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.

You SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:

* materially change the spec/plan, OR
* confirm/lock an assumption, OR
* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.
* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.

Use the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.

## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)

1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.

   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).
   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.
   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.
   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).

2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.

   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.
   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.
   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.

## Finalization rule

Only output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.

When you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:

1) The opening tag must be on its own line.
2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).
3) The closing tag must be on its own line.
4) Use Markdown inside the block.
5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.

Example:

<proposed_plan>
plan content
</proposed_plan>

plan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:

* A clear title
* A brief summary section
* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types
* Test cases and scenarios
* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed

When possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.

Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

Keep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.

Do not ask "should I proceed?" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.

Only produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.

If the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.
</collaboration_mode>

# Twenty v1.20 → v2.8 Upgrade Analysis

_Generated 2026-06-02. Fork: `github.com/u2giants/twenty`. Upstream: `github.com/twentyhq/twenty`._

---

## 1. Headline

Our fork branched from upstream at **v1.20.0** (merge-base `36dece43c7`, 2026-03-31) and has
merged **zero** upstream commits since. Upstream is now at **v2.8.0 + current main**.

- **1,297** upstream commits we are missing (a full major version + 8 minor releases)
- **230** custom commits of our own on top of v1.20.0
- A straight `git merge` produces **1,437 conflicts** and is **not viable** (see §6)
- Our core IP — the `pop-creations` module, email router, and re-router — merges with
  **zero conflicts** (net-new namespaced files)
- Recommended path: **re-fork from v2.8 and re-apply our customizations** (see §8)

---

## 2. Divergence facts

| Fact | Value |
|---|---|
| Fork base | `v1.20.0` + 20 commits (`36dece43c7`) |
| Upstream HEAD analyzed | `f6a17bc5f7` (2026-06-02) |
| Latest upstream release | `v2.8.0` (2026-05-26) |
| Commits upstream is ahead | 1,297 |
| Our custom commits | 230 |
| Files our commits touched | 4,220 (incl. tree-wide reformatting / footprint trimming) |
| Files upstream touched | 10,995 |

---

## 3. What upstream shipped that we don't have (v1.21.0 → v2.8.0)

Twenty publishes auto-generated PR lists, not narrative notes; the below is synthesized
from ~1,184 PR titles and the curated highlight bullets in later releases.

### AI / Agents (largest upstream investment)
- AI Chat promoted to a **public Lab feature** — nav menu item, redesigned model/tools/prompt tabs
- File attachments in agent chat; message compaction for long chats; "stop thinking when credits exhausted"
- AI SDK replaced with **GraphQL SSE streaming**; webhook tool providers; end-to-end observability
- `group_by_*` database tools, batch-tool preference, MCP tool annotations
- Standard skills backfill + availability messaging

### Email & Messaging (most relevant to our fork)
- **Send email from the UI** — inline reply composer + `SendEmail` mutation
- **Email attachments** + open-in-app click action; email thread widget on record pages
- **Email forwarding message channel**; workspace **toggle to sync internal emails**
- **Reply-account resolved from the thread's channel** (#20755) — smarter outbound
- **Sync-state reset when IMAP/SMTP/CalDAV credentials change** (#20405)
- **Channels indexed for per-workspace sync crons** (#20678) — faster sync at scale
- Dedicated **Email settings page** for handles & emailing domains (#21008)
- Recurring Google Calendar events

### Integrations & Apps platform
- **Fireflies** (meeting transcripts), **Slack workflow connector**, **GitHub connector** example app
- **Generic web search driver** (Exa support) with billing
- **Pre-installed apps** infrastructure, generic **OAuth provider** support, public-domain-to-app binding,
  app file storage, "not-vetted app" banners, npm packages settings
- SDK: `defineCommandMenuItem`, `definePageLayoutTab`, viewSorts in manifests,
  config-file as single source of truth, app-defined permission flags & unique fields

### Data model, filtering & record pages
- **Custom indexes UI** (manage indexes from Settings)
- **One-hop relation filters** in GraphQL API + **relation traversal in the filter dropdown**
- Search boxes in add-column / fields dropdowns; object icon colors
- **Page-layout system overhaul** — editable record page layouts, tab icon picker, widget
  side-panel management, rich-text & text-field widgets, new `TABLE_WIDGET` type, reset-to-default

### Admin, security & infrastructure
- **Admin panel**: chat viewer, read-only Billing tab, JWT signing-keys tab, server-upgrade status
- **JWT migrated to ES256** with key rotation (`secret-encryption:rotate` CLI)
- PKCE required for public OAuth; MCP/RFC-9728 OAuth alignment
- Stored-XSS, CSV/SMTP/SSRF security fixes
- **Maintenance-mode UI** + DDL lock; **upgrade tooling** (Prometheus gauges, structured logfmt,
  version validation); OTLP/Sentry observability
- ClickHouse-backed **metered credit caps** & billing events

---

## 4. Breaking changes we will hit on upgrade

1. **`ENCRYPTION_KEY` env var now required** — versioned envelope encryption for all at-rest
   secrets; `ConnectedAccount.connectionParameters` is now encrypted. Must be provisioned **before** deploy.
2. **`APP_SECRET` repurposed** — no longer used for approved-access-domain validation or session cookies.
3. **`permissionFlag` → `rolePermissionFlag`** rename + new permission-flag catalog and backfill.
4. **Channel objects relocated** (`messageChannel` / `messageFolder` / `calendarChannel`) — see §5.
5. **Deploy ordering** — some changes require deploying **server before front**.

---

## 5. The `messageChannel` / `messageFolder` / `calendarChannel` change

**What upstream did** (commit `76e144e85a`, PR #20836; migration command
`upgrade:2-8:drop-channel-standard-objects`): these three were **standard per-workspace CRM
objects** (visible in the data-model UI, queryable via GraphQL, with views & page-layouts).
They are now **core metadata-module entities** (`engine/metadata-modules/message-channel/…`) —
internal system tables. The upgrade command **drops the redundant workspace-schema copies**.
Sync functionality is unchanged; emails/calendars still sync exactly as before.

**Do we use them?** **No — not functionally.** Our routing operates entirely on
`message` / `_emailMessage`, `messageParticipant`, `company`, and `person`. There are
**zero functional references** to the three objects in our 230 commits (the only mentions are
in a passive metadata-schema snapshot JSON). Our router routes by *sender domain → company*, so
it never needed to know which inbox an email arrived through.

**Better / worse?**
- **Can we still do everything we do? Yes, fully.** We never built on the user-facing object.
- **Better:** the messaging subsystem we extend got real upgrades (send-from-UI, forwarding
  channel, internal-email sync toggle, reply-account-from-thread, credential-change sync reset,
  channel indexing for sync crons, dedicated Email settings page).
- **Worse / the real cost** is *upgrade mechanics*, not functionality:
  - **Merge-conflict hotspot** in messaging (upstream refactored it heavily) — see §7.
  - **One-way destructive migration** + our direct-to-prod habit: before upgrading, confirm no
    hand-made prod view/index/trigger references the old `workspace_…."messageChannel"` tables.
    (Our committed code does not — verified — but prod may hold ad-hoc objects the repo doesn't capture.)

| **Both-edited (real content conflicts)** | **170** | The genuine hand-merge work. |

## 8. Recommended strategy: re-fork-and-re-apply

A merge means adjudicating 1,264 files unrelated to our customizations. Instead, because our
work is cleanly separable:

1. **Start fresh from upstream `v2.8.0`** (or current main).
2. **Drop in the `pop-creations` module wholesale** — self-contained, zero conflicts. This is the
   bulk of our IP (router, re-router, 11 logic functions, AI opportunity summaries).
3. **Re-apply ~4 shared-file edits by hand** — the Gmail display-name feature and the NestJS
   logic-function wiring. Small, well-understood diffs.
4. **Re-apply the ~8 UI customizations** against v2.8's frontend — see §9 for the exact list.
5. **Skip the footprint-trimming entirely** — re-deleting packages/core files bought the
   1,264-conflict headache; do not re-do it without a concrete reason.
6. **Provision `ENCRYPTION_KEY`** and run the v2.x upgrade commands (incl.
   `upgrade:2-8:drop-channel-standard-objects`) on prod as part of cutover.

This converts a ~1,400-conflict merge into **porting one clean module + ~4 backend patches +
~8 UI re-applies**, landing on a current v2.8 base.

## 11. Confirmed: routing views are workspace data (prod DB check, 2026-06-02)

Queried prod (`docker exec twenty-postgres psql`, schema `workspace_93r34ew9zc9644a9y5f1yeylz`;
views live in **`core.view`**, scoped by `workspaceId`).

| View | Object | `core.view` id | Filter | Created | Repo-reproducible (before)? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Needs Routing** | company | `a1000002-…` | `customerStatus IS ["UNASSIGNED"]` | 2026-05-31 | No — created via UI/ad-hoc |
| **Unrouted** | emailMessage | `11111111-e1a1-…` | `routingStatus IS ["UNROUTED"]` (sort `receivedAt DESC`) | 2026-04-13 | No |
| **Unrouted** | meetingNote | `22222222-e1a1-…` | `company IS_EMPTY` | 2026-04-14 | No |
| No Company (was "Unassigned") | person | `44444444-e1a1-…` | `company IS_EMPTY` | via migration | Yes — `004` (insert) + `006` (rename) |

**Not duplicates.** The two "Unrouted" views live on **different objects** (emailMessage vs
meetingNote) with **different filters** and sit under different nav folders — they only share a
display name, so they never collide and there is nothing to de-duplicate. (An earlier draft of
this doc wrongly called them duplicates.)

**Fixed (drift resolved):** committed migration
`packages/twenty-server/src/modules/pop-creations/migrations/010_capture_routing_views.sql`
now captures all three views **and their sidebar placement** idempotently:
- view rows + filters + columns + the `receivedAt DESC` sort on the email view;
- the two formerly-"Unrouted" views **renamed** to **"Unrouted Emails"** / **"Unrouted Notes"**
  for sidebar clarity (with UPDATE statements that migrate the existing prod rows);
- the three nav folders (**Companies**, **Email**, **Meeting Notes**) — *find-or-create by name*
  so a re-fork reuses an existing folder rather than duplicating — plus the pinned VIEW items.

It uses name-based metadata lookups for portability and is fully idempotent (re-runs are no-ops).
Verified against prod: first run renamed the 2 views + 2 nav labels and created no duplicate rows
(3 views / 23 fields / 3 nav items / 3 folders unchanged); a second run was a pure no-op. On a
re-fork the migration reconstructs the complete set — views, filters, columns, sort, folders, and
sidebar items — from the repo.

> **Audience:** a developer who has *never* touched this codebase. Follow the phases in order.
> Do not skip verification gates. Every destructive step has a stated rollback. When a command's
> exact name can drift between versions, the step says **VERIFY** — confirm it before running.

## 0. Facts you need before touching anything

| Thing | Value |
|---|---|
| Production URL | `https://crm.designflow.app` |
| VPS (inspection/SSH only — never deploy via SSH) | `178.156.180.212` |

| Deployed image | `ghcr.io/u2giants/twenty:latest` |

## 1. Phase A — Safety net (do not skip)

**Step A1 — Full production database backup.**
```bash
docker exec -t twenty-postgres pg_dump -U twenty -d twenty -Fc \
  -f /tmp/twenty_preupgrade_$(date +%Y%m%d).dump
docker cp twenty-postgres:/tmp/twenty_preupgrade_$(date +%Y%m%d).dump ./backups/
```
**Verify:** dump file exists and is > 0 bytes. Copy it off the VPS.
**Rollback anchor:** this dump is the restore point for the entire upgrade.

**Step A2 — Snapshot current Coolify env vars** for app `rd261bt0wy7ifjrkoe1tkl92`
(export via Coolify UI/API). Save as `backups/coolify-env-preupgrade.txt`. You will diff against
this after adding `ENCRYPTION_KEY`.

**Step A3 — Record the current state:** note the running image digest, current git SHA
(`3a7298bf69` family), and take screenshots of the sidebar (folders + the 3 routing views) so you
can confirm parity after cutover.

**Gate:** do not proceed until A1's dump is verified and stored off-server.

## 2. Phase B — Build the new base branch

**Step B1 — Sync upstream and branch from the tag.**
```bash
git fetch upstream --tags
git switch -c v28-rebase v2.8.0      # if branch creation is blocked, do this in a clone/worktree
```
**Note:** this repo blocks branch creation on the server checkout. Do this work in a **separate
clone** or a Git worktree, then push the finished branch. Do **not** rewrite `main` until cutover.

**Step B2 — Confirm the base is clean v2.8.0** (`git describe --tags` → `v2.8.0`). This branch is
your new foundation. Everything below is re-applied **onto it**.

## 5-bis. Phase E-bis — Rebuild the 4 dead automations as native Workflows

> **Context:** the system is **pre-launch**; these four were specced but never worked (custom
> `@OnEvent` listeners that were never registered + misread the batched event payload — see §15).
> **Do NOT port the listener code.** Build them as native, **manifest-defined** Workflows in the
> POP Creations app so they are reproducible (not UI-clicked drift). The native Workflow engine
> already exists; this is replacing dead custom code with no-code definitions.

Build one workflow each (DATABASE_EVENT trigger → `create-record` / `update-record` action):

| Workflow | Trigger | Filter | Action(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Auto-create LAT | `opportunity` **updated** | `stage` ∈ {PROPOSAL, NEGOTIATION} & changed | Create `licensorApprovalThread`, relate to the opportunity |
| LAT follow-up task | `licensorApprovalThread` **updated** | `stage` ∈ follow-up set | Create `task`, relate to the LAT |
| New-program checklist | `opportunity` **created** | — | Create 6 `task`s (the existing template titles), each related |
| Contact auto-scope | `person` **created** | — | Set `scope` (use `if-else`/`filter` or a `code` action: has department → DEPARTMENT, else company → COMPANY_WIDE, else IGNORED) |

Source-of-truth for the exact rules (stage lists, the 6 task titles, scope logic) is the existing
listener code in `modules/pop-creations/logic-functions/{program-stage-change,lat-stage-follow-up,new-program-tasks,contact-auto-scope}/`
— **keep those files until the workflows are built and verified, then delete them.**

**Gate:** on staging, fire each trigger (move an opportunity to PROPOSAL, create a person, etc.) and
confirm the records/tasks/scope are created — i.e. the features work **for the first time**.

## 6. Phase F — Configuration & breaking changes (Coolify)

Do these in **Coolify** (runtime config), not in Git.

**Step F1 — `ENCRYPTION_KEY` (REQUIRED, breaking).** v2.x encrypts secrets at rest with a
versioned envelope key. Generate and add it **before** the first v2.8 boot:
```bash
openssl rand -base64 32
```
Add as env var `ENCRYPTION_KEY` on app `rd261bt0wy7ifjrkoe1tkl92`. **VERIFY** the exact var name
and format against v2.8 docs/`.env.example` — if it expects a versioned/JSON envelope, follow that
shape. Without it, connected-account credentials cannot be decrypted and email sync breaks.

**Step F2 — `APP_SECRET`.** No longer used for approved-domain validation / session cookies in
v2.x. Keep it set (still used elsewhere) but do not rely on it for those paths.

**Step F3 — `permissionFlag` → `rolePermissionFlag`.** Handled by an upgrade command (Phase G),
not manual SQL. Just be aware roles get a backfill.

**Gate:** Coolify env diff vs `backups/coolify-env-preupgrade.txt` shows `ENCRYPTION_KEY` added and
nothing unintended removed.

## 7. Phase G — Stage, build, run upgrades (on a NON-prod target first)

> **Strongly recommended:** do this against a staging copy restored from the A1 dump. If no staging
> exists, the same steps apply at cutover (Phase I) but with far less safety.

**Step G1 — Push the branch; let GitHub Actions build the image.** The workflow graph is
`lint → test → build/push image → deploy`. Confirm the image publishes to GHCR with an explicit,
auditable tag (not a floating `latest` for staging).

**Step G2 — Run Twenty's built-in version upgrade commands.** v2.x ships a versioned upgrade-command
framework that walks each version's migrations, including:
- `permissionFlag` → `rolePermissionFlag` rename + catalog backfill
- **`upgrade:2-8:drop-channel-standard-objects`** — drops `messageChannel`/`messageFolder`/
  `calendarChannel` from workspace schemas (they moved to core metadata). **This is the destructive
  one.** It does *not* touch our routing code (we don't use those objects — Part 1 §5/§7).

**VERIFY** the exact invocation in v2.8 (`package.json` scripts / docs). It is typically the
server's command runner, e.g.:
```bash
# VERIFY exact script name in v2.8 before running:
docker exec -i <server-container> yarn command:prod upgrade
```

**Step G3 — Apply our pop-creations SQL migrations in numeric order** (these are *our* migrations,
separate from Twenty's framework):
```bash
for f in $(ls packages/twenty-server/src/modules/pop-creations/migrations/0*.sql | sort); do
  echo ">> $f"
  docker exec -i <postgres-container> psql -U twenty -d twenty < "$f"
done
```
- `008` — `_emailMessage` routing index. **VERIFY** the `_emailMessage` table/column names still
  exist under v2.8's schema before applying; adjust if renamed.
- `010` — routing views + nav (idempotent; safe to re-run).

**Gate (staging):** see Phase H. Do not promote to prod until every check passes.

## 8. Phase H — Verification checklist (run on staging, repeat at cutover)

1. **App boots** — server and worker containers healthy; no decryption errors in logs.
2. **Connected accounts still sync** — trigger a manual sync; new emails import.
3. **Email routing works** — send a test email from a known customer domain; confirm the router
   assigns the right company/department (check the pop-creations router logs).
4. **Re-router cron runs** — confirm it processes and finds improvements (uses the `008` index).
5. **Routing views present & correct** — sidebar shows **Needs Routing** (Companies), **Unrouted
   Emails** (Email), **Unrouted Notes** (Meeting Notes); each lists the expected records.
6. **ParticipantChip** right-click opens the People record.
7. **Channel objects gone from data model** but sync unaffected (expected after `drop-channel`).
8. **Impersonation** works via the new v2.8 admin panel.
9. **No duplicate folders/views** — re-running `010` is a no-op:
   ```sql
   SELECT name,count(*) FROM core."navigationMenuItem"
   WHERE type='FOLDER' AND name IN ('Companies','Email','Meeting Notes')
   GROUP BY name;   -- each must be exactly 1
   ```

## 9. Phase I — Production cutover

**Step I1 — Fresh prod backup** (repeat A1; the staging run may be hours/days old).
**Step I2 — Set `ENCRYPTION_KEY`** in prod Coolify (Phase F) if not already.
**Step I3 — Deploy SERVER before FRONT.** Several v2.x changes require this ordering. Deploy the
server image via the Coolify API job, wait for healthy, then the front.
**Step I4 — Run Phase G2 (Twenty upgrades) then G3 (our migrations)** against prod.
**Step I5 — Run the full Phase H checklist against `https://crm.designflow.app`.**
**Step I6 — Verify BOTH `server-…` and `worker-…` containers** are on the new image and healthy.

**Rollback:** if any gate fails — redeploy the previous image digest (recorded in A3) via Coolify,
and if the destructive migration already ran, **restore the A1/I1 dump**:
```bash
docker exec -i twenty-postgres pg_restore -U twenty -d twenty --clean --if-exists \
  < backups/twenty_preupgrade_YYYYMMDD.dump
```
(The channel-drop is not reversible forward without restore — that is why the dump is mandatory.)

## 10. Phase J — Post-cutover

1. **Fast-forward `main`** to the rebased branch (this becomes the new fork base).
2. **Update memory + docs** — new base is `v2.8.0`; record the new merge-base for the *next* upgrade.
3. **Confirm no leftover drift** — the routing views/folders now come from migration `010`; nothing
   should exist in prod that isn't in the repo.
4. **Monitor** sync + routing for 48h; watch worker logs for decryption or migration warnings.
5. **Schedule the next upgrade sooner** — staying current from v2.8 is far cheaper than this jump
   was from v1.20.

## 11. Step count summary

11 phases (incl. Phase E-bis), ~30+ discrete steps. The irreversible ones are exactly two — **`ENCRYPTION_KEY` must be
set before first boot** (F1) and **the channel-drop migration** (G2) — both fully covered by the

## 12. Production drift audit (2026-06-02)

Goal: find everything that exists **only in production** (created via UI/API) and would be **lost
on a re-fork** because it is not reproducible from the repo. Audited `core.objectMetadata`,
`core.fieldMetadata`, `core.view`, `core.navigationMenuItem` against committed code + migrations.

### Data model — ESSENTIALLY CLEAN ✅
- **8 custom objects** (`aiModelConfig`, `department`, `emailMessage`, `factory`, `ignoreRule`,
  `licensorApprovalThread`, `meetingNote`, `meetingNoteAttendee`) are **fully code-defined** —
  workspace entities in `modules/pop-creations/standard-objects/` **plus** the standard-application
  metadata pipeline `engine/.../twenty-standard-application/utils/field-metadata/pop-creations/compute-*.util.ts`.
  Every field on all 8 objects was found in code — **zero drift**.
- **Custom fields on STANDARD objects** (opportunity ×23, company ×8, person ×7) are reproduced in
  code by **direct edits to upstream entities** (`company.workspace-entity.ts`,
  `person.workspace-entity.ts`, opportunity, + matching `compute-*-standard-flat-field-metadata.util.ts`).
- ⚠️ **One true field drift: `company.creditStatus`** — a `SELECT` (Good / Watch / Hold), created
  via UI, found **nowhere in code** (not even in a migration). Lost on re-fork unless captured.

> **Re-fork implication:** the data model is reproducible, but it lives in **modified upstream
> standard-entity files + the twenty-standard-application metadata utils** — NOT in the clean
> pop-creations module. v2.8 restructured this exact pipeline, so this is a **larger, conflict-prone
> re-apply surface than Part 1 §8 implied.** Part 2 Phase C/D must add: re-apply the company/person/
> opportunity field definitions and the `…/twenty-standard-application/utils/field-metadata/pop-creations/`
> compute utils. Treat this as the single biggest backend re-apply task.

### Views & nav — a TAIL OF DRIFT beyond the routing views ⚠️
The fork's `compute-standard-*-views.util.ts` only generate each object's default `All {label}`
view + a `Record Page Fields` view. Every **named/filtered** view below is therefore drift (not
code-generated, not in a migration), and would vanish on re-fork:

| Object | View(s) | Status |
|---|---|---|
| company | Needs Routing | ✅ **fixed** — migration `010` |
| emailMessage / meetingNote | Unrouted Emails / Unrouted Notes | ✅ **fixed** — migration `010` |
| person | **Company**, **Department**, **Person Fields Panel** | ✅ **fixed** — migration `011` |
| emailMessage | **Company**, **Dept.**, **Opport.** (grouping views) | ✅ **fixed** — migration `011` |
| meetingNote | **Company**, **Dept.**, **Opport.** (grouping views) | ✅ **fixed** — migration `011` |
| licensorApprovalThread | **All LATs** (kept) / **All Licensor Approvals** (deleted) | ✅ **fixed** — migration `011` |
| department | **All Departments ×2** | ✅ **de-duped** — migration `011` |
| factory | **All Factories ×2** | ✅ **de-duped** — migration `011` |

Genuinely standard (code-generated, NOT drift): `opportunity` "By Stage", `task` "Assigned to Me" /
"By Status", `person` "No Company" (migrations `004`/`006`), `workflowRun` "Runs",
`workflowVersion` "Versions", and every `All {label}` default.

### Drift inventory — remediation status (ALL RESOLVED)
| Item | Status |
|---|---|
| Routing views + nav (3 views, folders, pins) | ✅ migration `010` (applied + verified) |
| 9 grouping/panel views (email/meeting/person) | ✅ migration `011` (applied + verified) |
| 3 duplicate "POP Creations CRM" orphan views | ✅ deleted by migration `011` |
| `company.creditStatus` field | ⚪ intentionally **not** captured — unused; allowed to lapse on re-fork (per user) |

> **Third application discovered:** prod has **three** apps — `Twenty Standard`,
> `Workspace's custom application`, and **`POP Creations CRM`** (workspace-local, `sourcePath` is a
> UUID, **no repo manifest**). The deleted duplicates were empty default views it created.
>
> ⚠️ **DO NOT delete this app.** "No repo manifest" means its *registration row* isn't reproduced
> from code — NOT that it is junk. It is the **SDK host for live customizations**: 4 front components
> (**MondayMorningDashboard**, **ProgramFolio**, **DepartmentDashboard**, PersonDepartmentPicker),
> 2 record-page layouts (**Program Record Page**, **Department Record Page**), 4 widgets, 1 role,
> 1 command-menu item, 24 files. Three of the four dashboards are confirmed backed by repo code
> (`MondayMorningDashboardPage.tsx`, `widgetRegistry.tsx`, `DepartmentDashboardWidget.tsx`).
> Deleting the app would wipe all of that.
>
> **Re-fork implication (NEW drift layer, not yet captured):** the front-component *code* exists,
> but the **app row + its page layouts + widgets + command-menu item + role** are workspace data
> with no repo definition. They must be reproduced on re-fork — either by re-establishing this app
> via an SDK manifest, or by capturing the page-layout/widget rows in migrations (harder: v2.8
> reworked the page-layout system — see §9). `PersonDepartmentPicker` was not found by name in code;
> confirm whether it is renamed or genuine drift. Migrations `010`/`011` do NOT depend on this app
> (they resolve apps by name, falling back to `Workspace's custom application`).

**Bottom line:** the scary case — losing the custom data model — is **not** a risk; objects and
fields are in code (minus one unused field). All view/nav drift is now captured in migrations
`010`/`011` and verified idempotent against prod. A re-fork reconstructs the complete CRM —
data model from code, saved views + sidebar from `010`/`011`.

### Page layouts / front components owned by the POP Creations CRM app (audited 2026-06-02)
The app owns 4 `frontComponent` rows, 2 `pageLayout` rows + 4 tabs + 4 widgets, 1 command-menu
item, 1 role, 24 files. Findings:

- **Component code is all in the repo, consolidated** in `twenty-front/src/{pages,modules}/pop-creations`
  (`MondayMorningDashboardPage`, `DepartmentDashboardWidget`, `ProgramFolioWidget`, `widgetRegistry`).
  There is **no duplicate/SDK copy** in `packages/twenty-apps` to merge — nothing to consolidate.
- **`frontComponent` row usage:**
  - `DepartmentDashboard` (dfe52185) → referenced by the "Department Dashboard" widget — **used**
  - `ProgramFolio` (471f26c4) → referenced by the "Folio" widget — **used**
  - `MondayMorningDashboard` (982c22cd) → referenced by a **command-menu item** — **used, keep**
  - `PersonDepartmentPicker` (6c302daf) → **0 references, no code** → **vestigial, safe to drop**
    (its function was absorbed into the native relation/department picker work).
- ❌ **The 2 page layouts are DEAD — confirmed not rendering on any record.** Proof:
  `getRecordPageLayoutId.ts` resolves a record's layout from `record.pageLayoutId` (a per-record
  override) or a hardcoded object→default-layout map. **`department`/`factory`/`opportunity` are not
  in that map** (only company/person/opportunity/note/task/workflow…), and their record tables
  **have no `pageLayoutId` column** (only `dashboard` does). Combined with `objectMetadataId = NULL`
  on both layouts, nothing ever selects them. So the "Department Record Page" / "Program Record Page"
  layouts + their `DepartmentDashboard`/`ProgramFolio` widgets are **orphaned drift** — built (likely
  in the layout editor) but never wired to display.
  > **Heads-up:** the `DepartmentDashboardWidget` / `ProgramFolioWidget` you built are **not
  > currently shown** on department/program records. If you want them visible, that's a small
  > *new* wiring task in v2.8 (a `DefaultDepartmentRecordPageLayout.ts` in the existing pattern),
  > not drift to preserve. The only live custom front-component is **MondayMorningDashboard**
  > (command-menu item + native `/pop-creations` route — fully code-backed).

**Re-fork recommendation — do NOT capture page layouts in a SQL migration.** v2.8 reworked the
page-layout system (259 files); v1.20-shaped `pageLayout`/`pageLayoutTab`/`pageLayoutWidget` rows
(especially with NULL object linkage) will not port cleanly. The durable path is to reproduce the
dashboard mounting **in code** during the re-fork — either as code-defined record layouts
(the `Default*RecordPageLayout.ts` pattern, which exists for company/opportunity/note/task but not
department/program) or via the v2.x SDK app manifest (`definePageLayoutTab`). The mounting spec to
recreate: **Department record → "Overview" tab → DepartmentDashboard widget + "Activity" tab →
Timeline; Program record → "Folio" tab → ProgramFolio widget + "Activity" tab → Timeline.** The
component code already exists; only the placement needs re-expressing.

**Drift remediation — final status:** data model ✅ code; saved views/nav ✅ migrations `010`/`011`;
`creditStatus` ⚪ intentionally not captured (unused); `PersonDepartmentPicker` frontComponent
✅ dropped (migration `012`); the 2 dead page layouts + their widgets ⚪ harmless orphans (not
rendered) — re-mount in code at re-fork only if the dashboards are wanted; `MondayMorningDashboard`
✅ code-backed + reachable. **No remaining drift affects a re-fork's correctness.**
